title: What is a Fixed Deposit (FD)? — Complete 2026 Guide
meta_description: FD guide 2026 — current interest rates 6.5-7.5%, types of FD, tax on FD interest, premature withdrawal penalty, and FD vs PPF vs mutual fund comparison.

hero.one_liner: The simplest and safest investment in India — deposit a lump sum for a fixed period and earn guaranteed interest from 6.5% to 7.5% per annum

sections.heading: Overview
content: A Fixed Deposit (FD) is the most straightforward investment: you deposit a lump sum with a bank or NBFC for a fixed period (7 days to 10 years), and the bank pays you a guaranteed interest rate for that duration. Your principal is 100% safe and the interest is predetermined — no market risk, no fluctuation, no surprise.
This makes FDs ideal for risk-averse investors and for parking money you might need in 1-3 years.

Current FD interest rates (March 2026): Major banks offer 6.5-7.5% for general customers and 7.0-8.0% for senior citizens (60+). Small finance banks (AU Bank, Equitas, Ujjivan) and NBFCs (Bajaj Finance, Mahindra Finance) offer slightly higher rates (7.5-9%) but with marginally higher risk (they're not DICGC-insured).
SBI, HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank rates are typically in the 6.5-7.0% range for 1-3 year deposits. NRE FDs (for NRIs) offer 5.5-6.5% but the returns are tax-free in India.
FD interest is fully taxable at your income tax slab rate — this is the biggest disadvantage of FDs. If you're in the 30% tax bracket, your effective return after tax on a 7% FD is only about 4.8%.
This is lower than PPF (7.1% completely tax-free). However, for short-term parking (1-3 years) and emergency funds, FDs remain the safest and most liquid option available.

Types of FDs available: Regular FD (general), Tax Saver FD (5-year lock-in with 80C benefit), Senior Citizen FD (higher rate), Recurring Deposit (monthly deposits instead of lump sum), Flexi/Sweep FD (linked to savings account), and Corporate FD (from NBFCs).

rows.label: Interest Rate (2026)
rows.value: 6.5-7.5% (banks), 7.5-9% (NBFCs), +0.5% for senior citizens. Rates change frequently — check your bank's website for current rates.

rows.label: Minimum Deposit
rows.value: ₹1,000-10,000 (varies by bank). Most banks allow ₹1,000 minimum. Maximum is unlimited.

rows.label: Tenure Options
rows.value: 7 days to 10 years. Shorter tenures (7 days to 90 days) earn 5-5.5%. Medium (6 months to 1 year) earn 6-6.5%. Longer (3-5 years) earn 7-7.5%. Ultra-long (7-10 years) earn 7-7.5%.

rows.label: Tax on Interest
rows.value: Interest is fully taxable at slab rate. TDS deducted at 10% if annual interest >₹40,000 (₹50,000 for seniors). File ITR to claim refund if you're in lower bracket.

rows.label: Premature Withdrawal
rows.value: Allowed at most banks with 0.5-1% interest rate penalty. Some banks charge no penalty if closure is after 1 year. Read T&Cs carefully.

rows.label: DICGC Insurance
rows.value: Deposits up to ₹5 lakh per depositor per bank are insured (principal + interest combined). Keep deposits ≤₹5L per bank for safety.

rows.label: Section 80C Benefit
rows.value: Only 5-year Tax Saver FDs qualify for 80C deduction (up to ₹1.5L/year). Regular FDs do NOT get 80C benefit.

sections.heading: FD vs PPF vs Mutual Fund vs RD — Comparison
How to Maximize FD Returns — Smart Strategies
content:
Strategy 1: Ladder your FDs for better returns and liquidity

Instead of putting ₹10 lakh in one 5-year FD, split into ₹2 lakh each in 1-year, 2-year, 3-year, and 5-year FDs. As each matures, reinvest at the current rate.
This gives you better liquidity (one matures every year) AND captures rate increases (when rates go up, you reinvest at higher rates). Called the 'FD ladder' strategy.

Strategy 2: Compare rates across banks

Rates vary significantly — SBI and HDFC give lower rates because they're 'safe' names. Small finance banks like AU, Equitas, and Ujjivan often offer 0.5-1% higher.
For amounts up to ₹5 lakh, the DICGC insurance covers you even at smaller banks. Compare rates on BankBazaar, MyMoney, or check each bank's website.
Difference of 0.5% on ₹10 lakh for 5 years = ₹27,000 more interest.

Strategy 3: Senior citizens should always get higher rates

If you're 60+, always open FDs in the senior citizen category. The extra 0.25-0.50% rate adds up significantly.

On ₹10 lakh for 5 years, an extra 0.5% means ₹25,000+ more interest. Many banks offer additional benefits like accidental death benefit, critical illness coverage, or free locker for senior citizen FDs.

Strategy 4: Use Form 15G/15H to avoid TDS

If your total income is below the taxable limit (₹3 lakh in old regime, ₹3 lakh in new regime), submit Form 15G (if below 60) or Form 15H (if senior citizen) to the bank. This prevents TDS deduction on FD interest — you get the full interest without waiting for a tax refund.
TDS at 10% on ₹40,000 interest = ₹4,000 locked until ITR refund.

Strategy 5: Consider corporate FDs for higher returns

Bajaj Finance, Mahindra Finance, and other AAA-rated NBFCs offer 7.5-8.5% on FDs — significantly higher than bank FDs. However, these are NOT DICGC-insured.
Strategy: Only invest with AAA or AA+ rated companies (check CRISIL rating). Keep amounts reasonable (₹1-2 lakh per company).
Use corporate FDs only for surplus money you don't need immediately.

sections.heading: Premature Withdrawal and Penalties
content: Most banks allow premature withdrawal with a penalty. The penalty varies: Regular FD: 0.5-1% reduction in interest rate (some banks waive penalty after 1 year).
Tax Saver FD: CANNOT be prematurely withdrawn — that's the lock-in condition for 80C benefit. Breaking it means losing the 80C benefit for that amount.
Example: You have a 5-year FD of ₹1 lakh at 7%, earning ₹35,000 interest. You withdraw after 3 years.
Penalty: 1% of accrued interest = ₹1,050 reduction. You get ₹1 lakh principal + ₹20,000 interest (instead of ₹21,050) = ₹1,20,000 total.

Planning tip: If you might need money in 1-2 years, don't lock it in a 5-year FD. Instead: (1) Use a sweep FD (interest automatically converts to FD when it reaches threshold), (2) Keep 6 months in savings + rest in 1-year FD (ladder), or (3) Use a liquid mutual fund instead (instant redemption, slightly better returns).
For emergency funds: Split into multiple short-term FDs (3-6 months). As each matures, keep rolling 3-month FDs.
This gives liquidity while earning slightly better than savings account (5.5% vs 2.7%).

steps.title: Compare FD rates across banks
steps.description: Visit BankBazaar.com, MyMoney.com, or individual bank websites. Note the rates for your desired tenure and amount. Senior citizens should check senior citizen rates specifically.

steps.title: Open FD online (easiest) or at branch
steps.description: Most banks allow online FD opening through net banking or mobile app — takes 2 minutes. Select tenure (e.g., 3 years), amount (e.g., ₹5 lakh), and interest payout option (monthly, quarterly, or at maturity).

steps.title: Choose cumulative or non-cumulative option
steps.description: Cumulative: Interest added to principal, paid at maturity (better for growth). Non-cumulative: Interest paid monthly/quarterly to your account (better for regular income — useful for retirees living on interest).

steps.title: Submit Form 15G/15H if eligible
steps.description: If your total income is below taxable limit, submit Form 15G or 15H online to prevent TDS deduction. Available as online submission in most bank portals. This saves you from TDS lockup.

steps.title: Set auto-renewal reminder
steps.description: Set auto-renewal to avoid missing maturity date. Or set a phone reminder to review the FD at maturity — you can renew at the same bank if rates are still good, or switch to a higher-rate bank.

faqs.question: Is FD interest taxable?
faqs.answer: Yes, fully taxable at your income tax slab rate. If you're in the 30% bracket, you pay 30% + cess on all FD interest. TDS is deducted at 10% if your annual FD interest across all branches of a bank exceeds ₹40,000 (₹50,000 for seniors). You claim the actual tax or refund when filing ITR. Use Form 15G/15H to avoid TDS if you're below the taxable limit.

faqs.question: What happens if the bank fails?
faqs.answer: DICGC (Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation, a subsidiary of RBI) insures deposits up to ₹5 lakh per depositor per bank. This covers principal + interest combined. So if you have ₹6 lakh in a bank that fails, you get back ₹5 lakh (principal + interest combined). To stay safe, never keep more than ₹5 lakh in FDs at any single bank. If you have ₹10 lakh to invest, split: ₹5L at Bank A + ₹5L at Bank B.

faqs.question: Can I break my FD before maturity?
faqs.answer: Yes, premature withdrawal is allowed at most banks with a penalty of 0.5-1% reduction in applicable interest rate. Some banks charge no penalty if closure is after 1 year. Tax Saver FDs (5-year lock-in) CANNOT be prematurely broken — that's the condition for 80C benefit. Breaking a Tax Saver FD means you lose the 80C deduction benefit.

faqs.question: FD vs savings account — where should emergency fund go?
faqs.answer: Emergency fund (3-6 months expenses) should be in a combination: Keep 1 month expenses in savings account earning 2.7% (instant access). Keep 2-5 months in a sweep FD earning 6-6.5% (accessible in 1 day when needed). Or use a Liquid Mutual Fund (instant redemption, 6-7% returns). This way you earn reasonable returns while maintaining liquidity.

faqs.question: Can NRIs open FDs in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. NRIs can open NRE FD (Non-Resident External — tax-free in India, repatriable) or NRO FD (Non-Resident Ordinary — taxable, limited repatriability). NRE FDs are very popular among NRIs as the interest is completely tax-free in India — rates of 5.5-6.5% on INR deposits are attractive without the tax burden. Talk to your bank about NRE vs NRO options.

faqs.question: Which is better: FD vs PPF for long-term?
faqs.answer: PPF wins for long-term (5+ years) because: PPF gives 7.1% completely tax-free (EEE status), FD gives 6.5-7.5% but interest is fully taxable (effective ~4.5% after 30% tax). On ₹10 lakh for 15 years: PPF grows to ~₹27 lakh, FD grows to ~₹18 lakh (after tax). PPF lock-in (15 years) is not a problem if it's a long-term goal. FD is better for shorter-term (1-3 years) because you don't have to lock in for 15 years.

disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only. Consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.
